---
title: "What Is Probability? Sample Spaces and the Axioms"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Probability Foundations"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

POINT  = "#2b3a67"     # data / samples
LINE   = "#c1121f"     # the truth / theoretical value
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"     # highlights
GREY   = "#6c757d"
SETA   = "#3a7ca5"     # set A in Venn-style figures
SETB   = "#d98b2b"     # set B
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the **true entry point of the entire Core
> Sample series** — the ground floor of the Probability Foundations track, beneath
> Inference, Regression, Classification, and everything else. It assumes nothing above the
> Calculus-I floor: sets, functions, fractions, and the idea of a limit. It builds the
> objects every later article silently stands on — the **sample space**, the **event**,
> and the **axioms** that keep our probability assignments from contradicting one another.
> It sets up [PROB-1 *Conditional Probability, Independence & Bayes' Rule*] (how a
> probability changes when we learn something) and [PROB-2 *Random Variables*] (where a
> random variable becomes a *function on the sample space we build here*).

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

Before any formula for a mean, a variance, or a $p$-value, there is a prior question that
those formulas quietly assume an answer to: **what is the object we are assigning
probabilities to, and what rules must those assignments obey to never contradict
themselves?** This article answers it from scratch. We build the **sample space** $\Omega$
(the set of all outcomes) and the **events** (the subsets we bet on), develop the small
algebra of unions, intersections, and complements that combines them, and then state
**Kolmogorov's three axioms** — non-negativity, total mass one, and countable additivity.
From those three lines alone we *derive*, with every step shown, the rules every
practitioner uses on reflex: $P(\varnothing)=0$, the complement rule, monotonicity, and
inclusion–exclusion. The single claim this article defends is that **probability is not a
collection of formulas to memorize but the forced consequence of three axioms about
measuring sets** — and that the same three axioms hold whether you read probability as
counting equally likely cases, as a long-run frequency, or as a degree of belief.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
Fix a set $\Omega$ of all possible **outcomes** of an experiment. The things we assign
probabilities to are **events** — subsets of $\Omega$. A **probability** is a function $P$
that takes an event and returns a number, obeying just **three rules**: every event gets a
number that is at least $0$; the whole space $\Omega$ gets exactly $1$; and the
probability of a union of *disjoint* events is the *sum* of their probabilities. Every
identity in elementary probability — the complement rule, inclusion–exclusion, all of it —
is squeezed out of those three rules and nothing more.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The three rules look almost too modest to carry the
weight of a whole field, but by the end you should see them as inevitable: they are exactly
the conditions a *consistent way of measuring the size of sets* must satisfy, scaled so
that the biggest set measures $1$. The phrase "probability is a measure on sets" will stop
sounding like jargon and start sounding like a definition you could have written yourself.

# Why we are doing this: the problem that forced the axioms {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the entire article: **why does probability need axioms at all
— why not just compute?** Every move below is an answer to a piece of that question. If a
step ever feels like bureaucracy, return here.

## The original problem: gamblers, and a paradox of computing

Probability did not begin in a classroom. It began at a gambling table. In 1654 the French
writer and gambler Antoine Gombaud, the Chevalier de Méré, posed a puzzle to Blaise Pascal
about the fair division of stakes in an interrupted game of chance. Pascal's
correspondence with Pierre de Fermat that summer is usually marked as the birth of the
subject. Their method was concrete and unselfconscious: **count the equally likely
outcomes, and take the fraction that are favorable.** For dice and cards that recipe works
beautifully, and for two centuries probability was essentially the art of clever counting.

But "count the favorable fraction" carries a hidden assumption — that the outcomes really
are *equally likely* — and it has nothing to say the moment the outcomes are not equally
likely, or worse, when there are infinitely many of them. Ask for the probability that a
dart lands in the left half of a dartboard and the recipe stalls: there are infinitely many
landing points, each individually impossible to hit exactly, so "favorable outcomes over
total outcomes" is $\tfrac{\infty}{\infty}$, which is nonsense. By the late nineteenth
century the subject was riddled with paradoxes (Bertrand's, most famously) where two
"obviously correct" ways of counting gave two different answers to the same question. The
problem was not arithmetic. The problem was that **nobody had said what probability *is*** —
only how to compute it in the lucky finite case.

## Kolmogorov's move: stop computing, start axiomatizing

The fix, delivered by the Russian mathematician Andrey Kolmogorov in his 1933 monograph
*Grundbegriffe der Wahrscheinlichkeitsrechnung* ("Foundations of the Theory of
Probability"), was the same move Euclid made for geometry two thousand years earlier:
**stop arguing about what the objects "really" are, and instead write down the handful of
rules they must obey.** Kolmogorov declared that a probability is *any* assignment of
numbers to events that satisfies three axioms — and then everything else, including the
old counting recipe, becomes a *theorem* you derive, not an assumption you smuggle in.

This is the why we will hold onto for the rest of the article. The axioms exist to make
probability **consistent** — to guarantee that no two correct calculations can ever
disagree — and **general** — to cover the infinite, the unequal, and the continuous, not
just the dice table. Every axiom earns its place by ruling out a specific way the old
recipe could contradict itself. When we derive the complement rule or inclusion–exclusion
below, we are really checking that the three rules are rich enough to recover everything
the gamblers knew, and disciplined enough never to produce the paradoxes.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why axiomatize probability instead of just defining it as favorable-over-total?"* Because
favorable-over-total is not a definition — it is a *computation that only works under an
assumption* (equally likely outcomes) and *only in finite spaces*. It cannot price a
loaded die, a continuous measurement, or a subjective belief, and historically it produced
outright contradictions (Bertrand's paradox). Kolmogorov's axioms keep what works — they
*imply* favorable-over-total whenever outcomes are equally likely (we prove this in
@sec-classical) — while extending cleanly to every case the recipe could not touch, and
guaranteeing internal consistency throughout. The interviewer is checking whether you know
that the axioms are the *foundation* and the counting recipe is a *special-case theorem*,
not the other way around.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Before any axiom, hold one picture in mind: **probability is the measurement of area, with
the total area normalized to $1$.**

Imagine the set of all possible outcomes as a region — a rectangle of total area exactly
$1$. An event is a sub-region inside it. The probability of the event is *the fraction of
the total area it covers*. This single image makes all three axioms obvious before we even
state them:

- **No region has negative area.** (Non-negativity.)
- **The whole rectangle has area $1$** by construction. (Total mass.)
- **The area of two non-overlapping regions, taken together, is the sum of their two
  areas** — you do not double-count, and you do not lose anything in the gap, because
  there is no gap and no overlap. (Additivity for disjoint events.)

@fig-area draws this. Everything technical below is just making "area" precise enough to
survive infinite and continuous spaces — but the area picture is never wrong, and when a
formula confuses you, redraw it as areas.

```{python}
#| label: fig-area
#| fig-cap: "Probability as normalized area. The full square is the sample space $\\Omega$, with total area $1$. Two events $A$ (blue) and $B$ (orange) are sub-regions. The disjoint case (left): $A$ and $B$ do not touch, so the area of $A\\cup B$ is just $P(A)+P(B)$ — the additivity axiom. The overlapping case (right): they share a region (hatched), which gets counted once in $A$ and once in $B$, so naively adding double-counts it. Subtracting the overlap once is exactly inclusion–exclusion, derived in @sec-incexc."
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.6, 4.0))

for ax in (axL, axR):
    ax.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((0, 0), 1, 1, fill=False, edgecolor="black", lw=1.5))
    ax.set_xlim(-0.05, 1.05); ax.set_ylim(-0.05, 1.15)
    ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.axis("off")
    ax.text(0.5, 1.08, r"$\Omega$", ha="center", fontsize=13)

# Disjoint
axL.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((0.08, 0.2), 0.30, 0.45, color=SETA, alpha=0.55))
axL.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((0.58, 0.3), 0.30, 0.45, color=SETB, alpha=0.55))
axL.text(0.23, 0.42, "$A$", ha="center", fontsize=13)
axL.text(0.73, 0.52, "$B$", ha="center", fontsize=13)
axL.set_title("Disjoint: $P(A\\cup B)=P(A)+P(B)$", fontsize=11)

# Overlapping
from matplotlib.patches import Circle
cA = Circle((0.40, 0.45), 0.27, color=SETA, alpha=0.45)
cB = Circle((0.62, 0.45), 0.27, color=SETB, alpha=0.45)
axR.add_patch(cA); axR.add_patch(cB)
# hatch the overlap region approximately
axR.add_patch(Circle((0.40, 0.45), 0.27, fill=False, edgecolor=SETA, lw=1.2))
axR.add_patch(Circle((0.62, 0.45), 0.27, fill=False, edgecolor=SETB, lw=1.2))
axR.text(0.27, 0.45, "$A$", ha="center", fontsize=13)
axR.text(0.75, 0.45, "$B$", ha="center", fontsize=13)
axR.text(0.51, 0.45, r"$A\cap B$", ha="center", fontsize=8.5)
axR.set_title("Overlapping: subtract $P(A\\cap B)$ once", fontsize=11)

plt.tight_layout()
```

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

We build three layers, in order: the **sample space** (the outcomes), the **event algebra**
(combining outcomes into the things we measure), and finally the **axioms** (the
measurement itself). Nothing in a later layer is used before it is defined.

## Layer 1 — the sample space and outcomes {#sec-samplespace}

::: {#def-samplespace}
**Definition (sample space, outcome).** An **experiment** is any procedure whose result is
uncertain in advance. The **sample space** $\Omega$ (capital Greek omega) is the set of all
possible results of one run of the experiment. A single element $\omega \in \Omega$
(lowercase omega) is an **outcome** — one complete, indivisible result. The sample space
must be **exhaustive** (every possible result is in $\Omega$) and its outcomes
**mutually exclusive** (one run produces exactly one $\omega$).
:::

Read the definition through three experiments, so the abstraction has something to hold:

- **One coin flip.** A run yields heads or tails and nothing else, so
  $\Omega = \{\text{H}, \text{T}\}$. Two outcomes.
- **One roll of a six-sided die.** $\Omega = \{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$. Six outcomes.
- **A dart thrown at the interval $[0,1]$.** The landing point can be *any* real number
  between $0$ and $1$, so $\Omega = [0,1]$. Infinitely many outcomes, uncountably many —
  this is the case that broke the counting recipe, and we return to it in @sec-dart.

The symbol $\in$ means "is an element of"; $\omega \in \Omega$ reads "the outcome $\omega$
belongs to the sample space." The two requirements — exhaustive and mutually exclusive —
are not red tape. *Exhaustive* guarantees the experiment always lands somewhere in
$\Omega$, which is what lets us insist later that $\Omega$ has probability $1$. *Mutually
exclusive* guarantees a single run cannot be two outcomes at once, which is what lets us
*add* probabilities of distinct outcomes without double-counting.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"How do you choose the sample space — isn't it ambiguous?"* It is a **modeling choice**,
and the same experiment admits several valid $\Omega$'s depending on what you want to
track. Flip a coin twice: if you only care about the number of heads,
$\Omega=\{0,1,2\}$ suffices; if you care about the *order*,
$\Omega=\{\text{HH},\text{HT},\text{TH},\text{TT}\}$ is the right space. Neither is "wrong"
— but they answer different questions, and a subtle error in many probability problems is
silently switching between them. The rule of thumb: make $\Omega$ fine enough to express
every event you will ask about, and no finer.
:::

## Layer 2 — events and the algebra that combines them {#sec-events}

An outcome is one result. But we rarely bet on a single outcome; we bet on *collections* of
them — "the die shows an even number," "the dart lands left of center." Each such
collection is a subset of $\Omega$, and that is exactly what an event is.

::: {#def-event}
**Definition (event).** An **event** is a subset $A \subseteq \Omega$. We say the event
$A$ **occurs** on a given run if the outcome $\omega$ produced by that run lies in $A$
(that is, $\omega \in A$). Special cases: the **certain event** is $\Omega$ itself (always
occurs); the **impossible event** is the empty set $\varnothing$ (never occurs); a
**simple event** $\{\omega\}$ is a single-outcome subset.
:::

The symbol $A \subseteq \Omega$ ("$A$ is a subset of $\Omega$") means every element of $A$
is also in $\Omega$ — every outcome in the event is a genuine possible result. For the die,
"even" is the event $A = \{2,4,6\}$; it occurs precisely when the roll is $2$, $4$, or $6$.

Now the crucial bridge, and the reason set theory is the native language of probability:
**every English phrase joining events corresponds to a set operation, and vice versa.** We
build the three operations from scratch, then read each one back in plain words.

::: {#def-setops}
**Definition (the event algebra).** For events $A, B \subseteq \Omega$:

- The **complement** $A^c = \{\omega \in \Omega : \omega \notin A\}$ — all outcomes *not*
  in $A$. In words: "$A$ does **not** occur."
- The **union** $A \cup B = \{\omega : \omega \in A \ \text{or}\ \omega \in B\}$ — outcomes
  in $A$, in $B$, or in both. In words: "$A$ **or** $B$ occurs" (inclusive or).
- The **intersection** $A \cap B = \{\omega : \omega \in A \ \text{and}\ \omega \in B\}$ —
  outcomes in both at once. In words: "$A$ **and** $B$ both occur."

Two events are **disjoint** (or **mutually exclusive**) if $A \cap B = \varnothing$ — they
share no outcome, so they cannot both occur on the same run.
:::

The symbol $\notin$ is "is not an element of"; the $c$ superscript reads "complement." The
dictionary between logic and sets is worth tabulating once, because every probability
manipulation is secretly a translation across it:

| English (event language)      | Set operation     |
| ----------------------------- | ----------------- |
| $A$ does not happen           | $A^c$             |
| $A$ or $B$ (or both)          | $A \cup B$        |
| $A$ and $B$                   | $A \cap B$        |
| $A$ and $B$ cannot co-occur   | $A \cap B = \varnothing$ |
| if $A$ happens then $B$ does  | $A \subseteq B$   |

Two identities relating complement to union and intersection — **De Morgan's laws** — will
be used when we derive inclusion–exclusion, so we prove them now rather than assert them.

::: {#lem-demorgan}
**Lemma (De Morgan's laws).** For events $A, B \subseteq \Omega$,
$$
(A \cup B)^c = A^c \cap B^c, \qquad (A \cap B)^c = A^c \cup B^c .
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
We prove the first; the second is identical with "and"/"or" swapped. Two sets are equal
exactly when each is a subset of the other, so we show both inclusions by chasing a generic
outcome $\omega$ through the definitions.

*($\subseteq$)* Suppose $\omega \in (A\cup B)^c$. By the definition of complement,
$\omega \notin A \cup B$. By the definition of union, $\omega \in A \cup B$ would mean
$\omega$ is in $A$ or in $B$; since it is in neither, $\omega \notin A$ **and**
$\omega \notin B$. That is exactly $\omega \in A^c$ and $\omega \in B^c$, i.e.
$\omega \in A^c \cap B^c$.

*($\supseteq$)* Conversely suppose $\omega \in A^c \cap B^c$. Then $\omega \notin A$ and
$\omega \notin B$, so $\omega$ is in neither set, so it is not in their union:
$\omega \notin A \cup B$, i.e. $\omega \in (A\cup B)^c$.

Each set contains the other, so they are equal. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

In words, De Morgan's first law says: *"not ($A$ or $B$)" is the same as "(not $A$) and
(not $B$)."* If neither rain nor snow falls, then it did not rain **and** it did not snow.
That plain-English reading is the law; the set-chase above is only its careful proof.

## Layer 3 — the axioms {#sec-axioms}

We can now state what a probability *is*. Kolmogorov's three axioms assign to each event a
number, subject to the three constraints the area picture made intuitive.

::: {#def-axioms}
**Definition (Kolmogorov's axioms).** A **probability** is a function $P$ that assigns to
each event $A \subseteq \Omega$ a real number $P(A)$, called the probability of $A$,
satisfying:

- **(A1) Non-negativity.** $P(A) \ge 0$ for every event $A$.
- **(A2) Normalization.** $P(\Omega) = 1$.
- **(A3) Countable additivity.** For any sequence of events
  $A_1, A_2, A_3, \dots$ that are **pairwise disjoint**
  ($A_i \cap A_j = \varnothing$ whenever $i \ne j$),
  $$
  P\!\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} P(A_i) .
  $$
:::

Three remarks before we put the axioms to work.

**On (A1) and (A2): probabilities live in $[0,1]$.** Axiom (A1) forbids negative
probability — you cannot be *less than impossible*. Axiom (A2) fixes the total budget: the
certain event gets exactly $1$, so probabilities are *fractions of a fixed total*, the
normalized-area picture made literal. We will prove $P(A) \le 1$ for every event in
@sec-monotone; it is not assumed, it follows.

**On (A3): why *countable* additivity, and why disjoint?** The *disjoint* requirement is
the no-double-counting rule from the area picture: if two regions overlap, you cannot just
add their areas. The *countable* part — allowing infinitely many events $A_1, A_2, \dots$
indexed by the counting numbers — is the single feature that lets probability handle
limits, infinite series, and continuous spaces, which is precisely where the old counting
recipe failed. Finite additivity (just two, or any finite number, of disjoint events)
follows from (A3) as a special case; we extract it next.

**The pairwise-disjoint symbol.** "$A_i \cap A_j = \varnothing$ whenever $i \ne j$" means
*every distinct pair* shares no outcome — not merely that no single outcome is in *all* of
them, but that no outcome is in *any two* of them. This is what licenses summing without
overlap.

::: {.callout-note title="What we are flagging, and where it is built"}
Two technical points sit just above our Calc-1 floor, and honesty requires naming them
rather than hiding them.

*First:* for an infinite or continuous $\Omega$ (like the dart's $[0,1]$), one cannot
always assign a sensible probability to *literally every* subset — some subsets are too
pathological to measure consistently. The full theory restricts $P$ to a well-behaved
collection of events called a **$\sigma$-algebra**, and the study of which sets are
"measurable" is **measure theory**. For everything in this track we will only ever need
intervals, their countable unions, and ordinary combinations of those, all of which are
measurable — so we proceed safely and flag the subtlety here. We treat the line "$P$ is
defined on events" as covering exactly the events we actually use.

*Second:* the infinite sum $\sum_{i=1}^\infty P(A_i)$ in (A3) is the Calc-1 limit of
partial sums, $\lim_{N\to\infty}\sum_{i=1}^N P(A_i)$ — the same infinite series you met in
first-year calculus. No new machinery, just that limit.
:::

# Deriving the elementary rules from the three axioms {#sec-derived}

This is the payoff of axiomatizing: the rules every practitioner uses on reflex are not
extra assumptions. They are **theorems**, each squeezed out of (A1)–(A3) with nothing else.
We derive them in dependency order, every step on the page.

## Finite additivity {#sec-finite-add}

::: {#lem-finite-add}
**Lemma (finite additivity).** If $A$ and $B$ are disjoint events ($A \cap B = \varnothing$),
then $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$. More generally, for finitely many pairwise-disjoint
events $A_1, \dots, A_n$, $P(\bigcup_{i=1}^n A_i) = \sum_{i=1}^n P(A_i)$.
:::

::: {.proof}
Axiom (A3) is stated for an *infinite* sequence; we recover the finite case by padding with
empty sets. Take the two disjoint events $A, B$ and form the infinite sequence
$$
A_1 = A, \quad A_2 = B, \quad A_3 = \varnothing, \quad A_4 = \varnothing, \quad \dots
$$
These are pairwise disjoint: $A_1 \cap A_2 = A \cap B = \varnothing$ by assumption, and any
intersection involving an $\varnothing$ is $\varnothing$. Their union is
$A \cup B \cup \varnothing \cup \varnothing \cup \dots = A \cup B$, since unioning with the
empty set adds nothing. Apply (A3):
$$
P(A \cup B) = P\!\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^\infty A_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^\infty P(A_i)
= P(A) + P(B) + P(\varnothing) + P(\varnothing) + \cdots .
$$
We have not yet shown $P(\varnothing) = 0$ — that comes next, in @lem-empty. There is no
circularity, because @lem-empty's proof does **not** use this lemma: it applies axiom (A3)
*directly* to the all-empty sequence $\varnothing, \varnothing, \dots$ together with the
non-negativity axiom (A1), and nothing else. Both ingredients are axioms, available before
any lemma. Granting $P(\varnothing)=0$ for the moment, every tail term above vanishes and we
are left with $P(A\cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$. The general finite case pads $A_1, \dots, A_n$
with empty sets from index $n+1$ onward and repeats the argument verbatim. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

## The impossible event has probability zero {#sec-empty}

::: {#lem-empty}
**Lemma.** $P(\varnothing) = 0$.
:::

::: {.proof}
The empty set is disjoint from itself: $\varnothing \cap \varnothing = \varnothing$, since
there is no outcome in $\varnothing$ at all, let alone in the intersection. So we may apply
the *disjoint* additivity of (A3) to the sequence $\varnothing, \varnothing, \varnothing,
\dots$, whose union is $\varnothing$:
$$
P(\varnothing) = \sum_{i=1}^\infty P(\varnothing)
= P(\varnothing) + P(\varnothing) + P(\varnothing) + \cdots .
$$
Let $p = P(\varnothing)$. By (A1), $p \ge 0$. The equation says $p = p + p + p + \cdots$.
If $p > 0$, the right-hand side is an infinite sum of a fixed positive number, which
diverges to $+\infty$, while the left-hand side $p$ is finite — a contradiction. The only
non-negative value of $p$ making $p = p + p + \cdots$ hold is $p = 0$. Hence
$P(\varnothing) = 0$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

Notice this proof used only the *disjoint* additivity from (A3) and the non-negativity (A1)
— both raw axioms, with no appeal to @lem-finite-add. So the dependency runs one way only:
@lem-empty stands on the axioms alone, and @lem-finite-add then borrows its result. There is
no circle. With $P(\varnothing)=0$ now established, the tail terms in the finite-additivity
proof above genuinely vanish, completing it.

## The complement rule {#sec-complement}

::: {#thm-complement}
**Theorem (complement rule).** For any event $A$, $\;P(A^c) = 1 - P(A)$.
:::

::: {.proof}
The point of the complement is that an event and its complement *partition* the whole
space: every outcome is either in $A$ or not in $A$, never both. Formally, two facts.

First, they are **disjoint**: $A \cap A^c = \varnothing$, because no outcome can both be in
$A$ and (by definition of $A^c$) not be in $A$.

Second, they **cover** $\Omega$: $A \cup A^c = \Omega$, because every outcome
$\omega \in \Omega$ is either in $A$ or in $A^c$ — those are the only two options, and the
definition of $A^c$ puts every non-member of $A$ into it.

Apply finite additivity (@lem-finite-add) to the disjoint pair $A, A^c$ and then normalization
(A2):
$$
P(A) + P(A^c) = P(A \cup A^c) = P(\Omega) = 1 .
$$
Subtract $P(A)$ from both sides: $P(A^c) = 1 - P(A)$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This is the most-used rule in all of applied probability — "the chance it does *not* happen
is one minus the chance it does" — and it took three lines from the axioms. As an immediate
corollary, setting $A = \Omega$ gives $P(\Omega^c) = P(\varnothing) = 1 - P(\Omega) = 0$,
re-confirming @lem-empty from the other direction.

## Monotonicity and the upper bound $P(A) \le 1$ {#sec-monotone}

::: {#thm-monotone}
**Theorem (monotonicity).** If $A \subseteq B$ then $P(A) \le P(B)$. Consequently
$0 \le P(A) \le 1$ for every event $A$.
:::

::: {.proof}
The intuition is pure area: if region $A$ sits inside region $B$, its area cannot exceed
$B$'s. To make it exact, **carve $B$ into two disjoint pieces** — the part inside $A$, and
the part of $B$ outside $A$. The outside part is $B \cap A^c$ (in $B$, not in $A$). Claim:
$$
B = A \cup (B \cap A^c), \qquad \text{and these two pieces are disjoint.}
$$
*Disjoint:* $A \cap (B \cap A^c) \subseteq A \cap A^c = \varnothing$, since an outcome in
the second piece is in $A^c$, hence not in $A$. *Covering $B$:* take any
$\omega \in B$. Either $\omega \in A$ (first piece) or $\omega \notin A$, in which case
$\omega \in B$ and $\omega \in A^c$, so $\omega \in B \cap A^c$ (second piece). And we used
$A \subseteq B$ to know the first piece $A$ lies inside $B$, so the union does not overshoot
$B$. Now apply finite additivity:
$$
P(B) = P(A) + P(B \cap A^c) .
$$
By non-negativity (A1), $P(B \cap A^c) \ge 0$, so $P(B) = P(A) + (\text{something} \ge 0)
\ge P(A)$. That is monotonicity.

For the bound: every event satisfies $A \subseteq \Omega$, so monotonicity gives
$P(A) \le P(\Omega) = 1$. Combined with (A1)'s $P(A) \ge 0$, we get
$0 \le P(A) \le 1$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

So the familiar fact that *probabilities live between $0$ and $1$* was never an axiom — the
axioms gave us $\ge 0$ and $P(\Omega)=1$, and the rest is a theorem. This is the kind of
economy axiomatization buys: assume the minimum, derive the rest.

## Inclusion–exclusion: the overlap correction {#sec-incexc}

Finite additivity handles *disjoint* events. But events usually overlap, and the area
picture in @fig-area already warned us: adding $P(A) + P(B)$ when $A$ and $B$ overlap
counts the shared region $A \cap B$ *twice*. We now prove the correction exactly.

::: {#thm-incexc}
**Theorem (inclusion–exclusion, two events).** For any events $A, B$,
$$
P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B) .
$$ {#eq-incexc2}
:::

::: {.proof}
The strategy is to express each of $A \cup B$, $A$, and $B$ as unions of **disjoint** pieces
so that finite additivity (@lem-finite-add) applies cleanly, then combine. Split the plane
into three disjoint regions using $A$, $B$, and their overlap:

- $A \cap B^c$ — in $A$ only,
- $A^c \cap B$ — in $B$ only,
- $A \cap B$ — in both.

These three are pairwise disjoint (each forces a different membership pattern, so no outcome
lies in two of them), and we now write the events we care about in terms of them.

*Decompose $A$.* Every outcome of $A$ is either also in $B$ or not:
$$
A = (A \cap B^c) \cup (A \cap B), \qquad \text{disjoint},
$$
so finite additivity gives
$$
P(A) = P(A \cap B^c) + P(A \cap B). \tag{i}
$$
*Decompose $B$* the same way:
$$
B = (A^c \cap B) \cup (A \cap B), \quad\text{disjoint}, \quad\Longrightarrow\quad
P(B) = P(A^c \cap B) + P(A \cap B). \tag{ii}
$$
*Decompose $A \cup B$* into the three disjoint regions (an outcome in $A \cup B$ is in $A$
only, in $B$ only, or in both):
$$
A \cup B = (A \cap B^c) \cup (A^c \cap B) \cup (A \cap B), \quad\text{pairwise disjoint},
$$
so
$$
P(A \cup B) = P(A \cap B^c) + P(A^c \cap B) + P(A \cap B). \tag{iii}
$$
Now combine. From (i), $P(A \cap B^c) = P(A) - P(A \cap B)$; from (ii),
$P(A^c \cap B) = P(B) - P(A \cap B)$. Substitute both into (iii):
$$
P(A \cup B) = \bigl[P(A) - P(A \cap B)\bigr] + \bigl[P(B) - P(A \cap B)\bigr] + P(A \cap B).
$$
Collect the three copies of $P(A \cap B)$ — there are $-1 -1 +1 = -1$ of them:
$$
P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B),
$$
which is @eq-incexc2. The overlap, double-counted by $P(A)+P(B)$, is subtracted off exactly
once. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The pattern generalizes: for three events you add the singles, subtract the pairwise
overlaps, and add back the triple overlap (which got over-subtracted), and so on with
alternating signs. Rather than assert the three-event form, we **derive it in full** — and
the only tool we need is the two-event identity @eq-incexc2 we just proved, applied twice.

::: {#thm-incexc3}
**Theorem (inclusion–exclusion, three events).** For any events $A, B, C$,
$$
P(A \cup B \cup C) = P(A)+P(B)+P(C) - P(A\cap B) - P(A\cap C) - P(B\cap C) + P(A\cap B\cap C).
$$ {#eq-incexc3}
:::

::: {.proof}
The trick is to **group** the three-fold union as a two-fold union and reuse @eq-incexc2.
Write $A \cup B \cup C = (A \cup B) \cup C$ and treat $A \cup B$ as a single event. Applying
the two-event rule @eq-incexc2 to the pair $(A\cup B)$ and $C$,
$$
P(A \cup B \cup C) = P(A \cup B) + P(C) - P\bigl((A \cup B) \cap C\bigr). \tag{1}
$$
We attack the two non-trivial terms in turn.

*The first term* expands by @eq-incexc2 applied to $A$ and $B$:
$$
P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B). \tag{2}
$$

*The third term* needs the intersection $(A \cup B) \cap C$ rewritten. Set theory gives the
**distributive law** $(A \cup B) \cap C = (A \cap C) \cup (B \cap C)$ — an outcome is in the
left side exactly when it is in $C$ and in ($A$ or $B$), which is exactly when it is in
($A$ and $C$) or in ($B$ and $C$), the right side. (We verify it by the same
outcome-chasing as @lem-demorgan: $\omega \in (A\cup B)\cap C$ iff $\omega\in C$ and
$\omega\in A\cup B$, iff $\omega\in C$ and ($\omega\in A$ or $\omega\in B$), iff
($\omega\in A\cap C$) or ($\omega\in B\cap C$), iff $\omega \in (A\cap C)\cup(B\cap C)$.)
Now apply @eq-incexc2 *again*, this time to the pair $A\cap C$ and $B\cap C$:
$$
P\bigl((A \cup B) \cap C\bigr)
= P(A \cap C) + P(B \cap C) - P\bigl((A \cap C) \cap (B \cap C)\bigr). \tag{3}
$$
The last intersection collapses: $(A \cap C) \cap (B \cap C) = A \cap B \cap C$, because an
outcome in both $A\cap C$ and $B\cap C$ is in $A$, $B$, **and** $C$ (the repeated $C$ is
absorbed). So (3) becomes
$$
P\bigl((A \cup B) \cap C\bigr) = P(A \cap C) + P(B \cap C) - P(A \cap B \cap C). \tag{3$'$}
$$
Substitute (2) and (3$'$) back into (1):
$$
P(A \cup B \cup C)
= \underbrace{\bigl[P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)\bigr]}_{(2)} + P(C)
- \underbrace{\bigl[P(A \cap C) + P(B \cap C) - P(A \cap B \cap C)\bigr]}_{(3')}.
$$
Distribute the leading minus sign across the bracketed third term — it flips each of its
three signs — and collect:
$$
P(A \cup B \cup C) = P(A)+P(B)+P(C) - P(A\cap B) - P(A\cap C) - P(B\cap C) + P(A\cap B\cap C),
$$
which is @eq-incexc3. Every overlap is now corrected exactly: the three pairwise
intersections are each subtracted once, and the triple intersection — over-subtracted by
those three pairwise terms — is added back once. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

@fig-incexc shows why each sign is what it is: the triple-overlap region is added three
times by the singles, removed three times by the pairs, and so must be added back once to
be counted correctly. This is the bookkeeping the algebra above made exact.

```{python}
#| label: fig-incexc
#| fig-cap: "Inclusion–exclusion for three events, by counting how many times each region is tallied. With three overlapping circles, the central region (in all three) is added $+3$ by the single terms $P(A),P(B),P(C)$, then removed $-3$ by the three pairwise terms, leaving it at $0$ — so it must be added back once by $+P(A\\cap B\\cap C)$ to be counted exactly once. Each pairwise-only lens is added $+2$ and removed $-1$, netting the correct $+1$. The alternating signs of @eq-incexc3 are exactly this bookkeeping."
from matplotlib.patches import Circle
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.6, 5.2))
r = 0.62
centers = {"A": (0.40, 0.62), "B": (0.60, 0.62), "C": (0.50, 0.42)}
colors = {"A": SETA, "B": SETB, "C": ACCENT}
for name, (cx, cy) in centers.items():
    ax.add_patch(Circle((cx, cy), r, color=colors[name], alpha=0.30,
                        edgecolor=colors[name], lw=1.5))
ax.text(0.10, 0.92, "$A$", fontsize=14, color=SETA)
ax.text(0.90, 0.92, "$B$", fontsize=14, color=SETB)
ax.text(0.50, 0.05, "$C$", fontsize=14, color=ACCENT)
ax.text(0.50, 0.55, "+1", ha="center", fontsize=10, fontweight="bold")
ax.text(0.50, 0.55 - 0.0, "", ha="center")
ax.set_xlim(-0.3, 1.3); ax.set_ylim(-0.2, 1.5)
ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.axis("off")
ax.set_title("Each region counted exactly once", fontsize=12)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When does $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$ hold exactly?"* Precisely when $P(A \cap B) = 0$ —
which includes the disjoint case $A \cap B = \varnothing$ but is slightly more general (the
overlap can be nonempty yet have zero probability, as happens with single points in a
continuous space, @sec-dart). The classic interview trap is assuming
$P(A \cup B) = P(A)+P(B)$ for *any* two events; it over-counts the overlap and can even
produce a "probability" exceeding $1$. The fix is always @eq-incexc2.
:::

# The three interpretations — and why the axioms hold for all of them {#sec-interp}

Here is something the axioms quietly accomplish that is easy to miss: they let probability
*mean* whatever your problem needs it to mean, while the mathematics stays identical. There
are three honest interpretations of the number $P(A)$, and the entire field rests on the
fact that **all three satisfy (A1)–(A3)** — so every theorem we derived applies no matter
which you hold. This is what lets the Probability track stay neutral and the downstream
tracks each pick the interpretation their subject demands.

## Classical (equally likely outcomes) {#sec-classical}

When $\Omega$ is finite with $N$ equally likely outcomes, the classical definition is the
gamblers' recipe:
$$
P(A) = \frac{|A|}{|\Omega|} = \frac{\text{number of outcomes in } A}{\text{total number of outcomes}},
$$ {#eq-classical}
where $|A|$ denotes the count of outcomes in $A$. We promised in @sec-why that this is now a
*theorem*, not an assumption — let us see it fall out of the axioms.

"Equally likely" means each of the $N$ simple events $\{\omega_1\}, \dots, \{\omega_N\}$ has
the same probability, call it $q$. They are disjoint (distinct outcomes) and their union is
all of $\Omega$, so finite additivity and normalization give
$$
1 = P(\Omega) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} P(\{\omega_i\}) = \sum_{i=1}^N q = Nq
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad q = \frac{1}{N}.
$$
Now any event $A$ with $|A| = k$ outcomes is the disjoint union of its $k$ simple events, so
additivity again gives $P(A) = k \cdot q = k/N = |A|/|\Omega|$, which is exactly
@eq-classical. The recipe is recovered — but *only* under the equally-likely assumption,
made explicit, and *only* for finite $\Omega$.

## Frequentist (long-run relative frequency) {#sec-frequentist}

The frequentist reads $P(A)$ as the limiting fraction of runs in which $A$ occurs, over
indefinitely many independent repetitions:
$$
P(A) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n_A}{n},
$$
where $n_A$ is the number of the first $n$ runs on which $A$ occurred. A relative frequency
$n_A/n$ is automatically $\ge 0$ (A1), equals $1$ when $A=\Omega$ since $\Omega$ occurs
every run (A2), and adds across disjoint events because the counts $n_A$ do — if $A$ and
$B$ cannot co-occur, then $n_{A\cup B} = n_A + n_B$ on every run, so the limiting fractions
add (A3). Whether that limit actually exists is the content of the **Law of Large Numbers**,
a *theorem* about samples that the Statistical Inference track owns (it is built from the
Chebyshev inequality of [PROB-19]) — here we only note that *if* probabilities behave like
long-run frequencies, they obey the axioms. @fig-freq shows frequencies settling toward the
classical value.

```{python}
#| label: fig-freq
#| fig-cap: "The frequentist picture, simulated. We repeatedly roll a fair die and track the running relative frequency of the event $A=\\{$even$\\}$. Three independent runs (colored) each wander early but converge to the classical value $P(A)=3/6=0.5$ (red line). The axioms hold for the frequency $n_A/n$ at every $n$ — it is always in $[0,1]$ and adds over disjoint events — and the Law of Large Numbers (owned by Inference) is what guarantees the limit exists. This is *why* the classical and frequentist numbers agree for a fair die."
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n_max = 2000
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
for c in (POINT, ACCENT, SETB):
    rolls = rng.integers(1, 7, size=n_max)
    is_even = (rolls % 2 == 0).astype(float)
    running = np.cumsum(is_even) / np.arange(1, n_max + 1)
    ax.plot(np.arange(1, n_max + 1), running, color=c, lw=1.3, alpha=0.85)
ax.axhline(0.5, color=LINE, lw=1.8, label="classical $P(A)=0.5$")
ax.set_xscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel("number of rolls $n$ (log scale)")
ax.set_ylabel("relative frequency of 'even'")
ax.set_title("Frequencies converge to the axiomatic probability")
ax.set_ylim(0, 1)
ax.legend(loc="upper right", framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Subjective (degree of belief) {#sec-subjective}

The subjectivist (Bayesian) reads $P(A)$ as a personal *degree of belief* that $A$ will
occur — the fair price you would pay for a ticket that returns \$1 if $A$ happens. Why must
a coherent belief obey the axioms? Because if it did not, you could be made to lose for
certain. This is the **Dutch book** argument: if your stated probabilities violated, say,
the complement rule (you priced $A$ and $A^c$ to sum to more than $1$), a bookmaker could
sell you bets on both and pocket the difference no matter what happens. Avoiding a
guaranteed loss *forces* your degrees of belief to satisfy (A1)–(A3). So even a number that
lives entirely in your head must obey Kolmogorov, on pain of incoherence.

::: {.callout-important title="Why this matters for the whole series"}
The axioms are the **neutral ground** the entire Core Sample series stands on. Because all
three interpretations satisfy (A1)–(A3), every theorem in this track — expectation,
variance, the named distributions, the limit machinery — is true under *all* of them, and
no downstream track has to commit to one before it must. Regression and classical Inference
lean frequentist; Bayesian Inference and the Causal track lean subjective; the gambler's
classical view seeds the combinatorics of [PROB-10]. They differ on *what the number means*,
never on *how the numbers combine*. That separation is exactly what Kolmogorov bought us.
:::

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We now run the machinery on concrete experiments — finite and equally likely, finite and
*not* equally likely, and finally the continuous case that broke the old recipe and forced
the leap to measuring sets.

## Example 1 — two dice, by hand and by axiom {#sec-twodice}

Roll two fair six-sided dice. We want $P(\text{sum} \ge 10)$ and, to exercise
inclusion–exclusion, $P(\text{sum} \ge 10 \ \text{or a double})$, where a "double" is two
matching faces.

The sample space tracks the ordered pair of faces, so
$\Omega = \{(i,j) : i, j \in \{1,\dots,6\}\}$, with $|\Omega| = 6 \times 6 = 36$ equally
likely outcomes (each die is fair and the two are physically independent, so every ordered
pair is as likely as any other). We build the events as sets and count.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-dice
#| echo: false
import itertools
import numpy as np

omega = list(itertools.product(range(1, 7), range(1, 7)))
N = len(omega)

A = [(i, j) for (i, j) in omega if i + j >= 10]      # sum >= 10
B = [(i, j) for (i, j) in omega if i == j]           # a double
AorB = [(i, j) for (i, j) in omega if (i + j >= 10) or (i == j)]
AandB = [(i, j) for (i, j) in omega if (i + j >= 10) and (i == j)]

print(f"|Omega|            = {N}")
print(f"|A| (sum >= 10)    = {len(A)}   ->  P(A)        = {len(A)}/36 = {len(A)/N:.4f}")
print(f"|B| (a double)     = {len(B)}   ->  P(B)        = {len(B)}/36 = {len(B)/N:.4f}")
print(f"|A and B|          = {len(AandB)}   ->  P(A cap B)  = {len(AandB)}/36 = {len(AandB)/N:.4f}")
print(f"|A or B|  (direct count)         =  {len(AorB)}")
incexc = len(A) + len(B) - len(AandB)
print(f"inclusion-exclusion |A|+|B|-|AcapB| = {len(A)}+{len(B)}-{len(AandB)} = {incexc}")
print(f"P(A or B) = {len(AorB)}/36 = {len(AorB)/N:.4f}")
```

Read the counts the code printed straight through the rules:

- $A = \{\text{sum} \ge 10\}$ contains the pairs summing to $10, 11, 12$: that is
  $\{(4,6),(5,5),(6,4)\}$, $\{(5,6),(6,5)\}$, $\{(6,6)\}$ — six outcomes, so by the
  classical formula @eq-classical, $P(A) = 6/36 = 1/6$.
- $B = \{\text{double}\}$ is $\{(1,1),\dots,(6,6)\}$, six outcomes, $P(B) = 6/36 = 1/6$.
- Their overlap $A \cap B$ is the doubles that also sum to $\ge 10$: only $(5,5)$ and
  $(6,6)$, so $P(A \cap B) = 2/36$.
- Inclusion–exclusion @eq-incexc2 then gives
  $P(A \cup B) = \tfrac{6}{36} + \tfrac{6}{36} - \tfrac{2}{36} = \tfrac{10}{36}$, which
  matches the direct count of $|A \cup B| = 10$ exactly. Had we naively added $6+6=12$ we
  would have double-counted $(5,5)$ and $(6,6)$.

The point is not the arithmetic but the *agreement*: the derived rule @eq-incexc2 and a
brute-force count of the union give the identical answer, as a correct axiom system
guarantees they must. @fig-dice shows the $6\times 6$ grid with the events shaded.

```{python}
#| label: fig-dice
#| fig-cap: "The sample space of two dice as a $6\\times 6$ grid of $36$ equally likely outcomes. Shaded: the event 'sum $\\ge 10$' (blue) and 'a double' (orange diagonal). The two cells where they overlap — $(5,5)$ and $(6,6)$ — are the hatched squares counted twice if you naively add, and subtracted once by inclusion–exclusion. Probability here is literally *fraction of shaded cells*."
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.4, 5.4))
for i in range(1, 7):
    for j in range(1, 7):
        face = "white"
        if i + j >= 10 and i == j:
            face = "#9b5de5"          # overlap
        elif i + j >= 10:
            face = SETA
        elif i == j:
            face = SETB
        ax.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((i - 0.5, j - 0.5), 1, 1,
                     facecolor=face, edgecolor="white", lw=1.2, alpha=0.7))
ax.set_xlim(0.5, 6.5); ax.set_ylim(0.5, 6.5)
ax.set_xticks(range(1, 7)); ax.set_yticks(range(1, 7))
ax.set_xlabel("die 1"); ax.set_ylabel("die 2")
ax.set_aspect("equal")
ax.set_title("Blue: sum $\\geq 10$ · Orange: double · Purple: both")
ax.grid(False)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Example 2 — a loaded die, where counting fails but the axioms do not {#sec-loaded}

Now the honest case that the classical recipe *cannot* touch: a **loaded** die, where the
six faces are not equally likely. Suppose a casino-grade weighted die has face
probabilities
$$
p_1 = 0.10,\ p_2 = 0.10,\ p_3 = 0.15,\ p_4 = 0.15,\ p_5 = 0.20,\ p_6 = 0.30.
$$
The counting formula @eq-classical is simply *wrong* here — there is no "favorable over
total," because the faces are not interchangeable. But the axioms are untouched: any
assignment of non-negative numbers to the simple events that sums to $1$ is a legitimate
probability, and event probabilities are sums over their outcomes by finite additivity.

```{python}
#| label: fig-loaded
#| fig-cap: "A loaded die. **Left:** the probability mass on each face — non-equal, but non-negative and summing to $1$, so a valid probability by the axioms. **Right:** the event 'roll $\\ge 5$' has probability $p_5+p_6=0.50$ by finite additivity (@lem-finite-add), shaded. The classical recipe $|A|/|\\Omega| = 2/6 \\approx 0.33$ would be the *wrong* answer (red dashed). The axioms handle the unequal case the counting recipe cannot."
faces = np.arange(1, 7)
p = np.array([0.10, 0.10, 0.15, 0.15, 0.20, 0.30])
assert abs(p.sum() - 1.0) < 1e-12   # axiom A2 check

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.8))
axL.bar(faces, p, color=POINT, alpha=0.85)
axL.set_xlabel("face"); axL.set_ylabel("probability $p_i$")
axL.set_title(f"Loaded die (sums to {p.sum():.2f})")
axL.set_ylim(0, 0.35)

colors = [SETA if f >= 5 else GREY for f in faces]
axR.bar(faces, p, color=colors, alpha=0.85)
P_ge5 = p[faces >= 5].sum()
axR.axhline(0, color="black", lw=0.8)
axR.set_xlabel("face"); axR.set_ylabel("probability")
axR.set_title(f"$P(\\geq 5) = p_5+p_6 = {P_ge5:.2f}$")
axR.text(3.5, 0.27, f"classical (wrong):\n2/6 = {2/6:.2f}", color=LINE, fontsize=9)
axR.axhline(2/6, color=LINE, ls="--", lw=1.4)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"P(roll >= 5) = p5 + p6 = {p[4]:.2f} + {p[5]:.2f} = {P_ge5:.2f}")
print(f"P(roll is even) = p2+p4+p6 = {p[1]+p[3]+p[5]:.2f}")
print(f"check A2: sum of all face probabilities = {p.sum():.4f}")
```

This is the everyday reality of applied probability: outcomes are almost never equally
likely. A customer either converts or does not — but not with probability $1/2$. A packet
either drops or arrives — but not fifty-fifty. The axiomatic definition is what lets us
assign *whatever* the data say the probabilities are, and still combine them with the
complement rule and inclusion–exclusion exactly as before. The counting recipe was a
special case all along.

## Example 3 — a dart on $[0,1]$: the leap to measuring sets {#sec-dart}

Now the experiment that forced the whole edifice: throw a dart that lands uniformly at
random somewhere in the interval $[0,1]$. The sample space is *uncountable*,
$\Omega = [0,1]$, and the old recipe collapses — "favorable outcomes over total outcomes"
is $\tfrac{\infty}{\infty}$.

The axioms rescue us by shifting attention from outcomes to **sets**. "Uniform" means the
probability of landing in a sub-interval is proportional to its *length*, and since the
whole interval $[0,1]$ has length $1$ and must get probability $1$ (A2), the proportionality
constant is $1$:
$$
P([a,b]) = b - a, \qquad 0 \le a \le b \le 1 .
$$ {#eq-uniform}
Length is exactly the "area" of the intuition section, now in one dimension. Length is
non-negative (A1), the whole interval has length $1$ (A2), and the lengths of disjoint
sub-intervals add (A3) — so @eq-uniform *is* a probability in Kolmogorov's sense.

Here is the famous consequence, and it is not a paradox once you have the axioms. **The
probability of hitting any single exact point is zero.** Fix a point $c \in [0,1]$. The
singleton $\{c\}$ is contained in the interval $[c, c+\varepsilon]$ for every
$\varepsilon > 0$, so by monotonicity (@thm-monotone) and @eq-uniform,
$$
0 \le P(\{c\}) \le P([c, c+\varepsilon]) = (c+\varepsilon) - c = \varepsilon .
$$
This holds for *every* $\varepsilon > 0$, however tiny. The only number that is squeezed
between $0$ and every positive $\varepsilon$ is $0$ itself, so $P(\{c\}) = 0$. (This is the
Calc-1 squeeze, applied to a constant: a non-negative quantity below every positive number
must be zero.)

This dissolves the apparent contradiction — "the dart lands *somewhere*, yet every single
landing point has probability $0$" — that troubled pre-Kolmogorov probability. There is no
contradiction: an *uncountable* union of zero-probability points is **not** governed by
additivity, because (A3) only sums over *countable* sequences. You cannot add up
uncountably many zeros to recover the length. The probability lives in the *sets* (intervals
have length), not in the individual points (which have none). @fig-dart makes the squeeze
visible.

```{python}
#| label: fig-dart
#| fig-cap: "The continuous leap. A dart lands uniformly on $[0,1]$, so $P([a,b]) = b-a$ (length). **Top:** the event $[0.30,0.55]$ has probability $0.25$ — its shaded length. **Bottom:** a single point $c=0.4$ is trapped inside intervals $[c, c+\\varepsilon]$ of shrinking length $\\varepsilon$; since $P(\\{c\\}) \\le \\varepsilon$ for *every* $\\varepsilon>0$, the squeeze forces $P(\\{c\\})=0$. Probability is carried by sets of positive length, not by points."
fig, (axT, axB) = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(7.2, 4.2))

# Top: interval event
axT.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((0, 0), 1, 1, facecolor="none", edgecolor="black", lw=1.3))
axT.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((0.30, 0), 0.25, 1, facecolor=SETA, alpha=0.55))
axT.text(0.425, 0.5, "$P([0.30,0.55])=0.25$", ha="center", va="center", fontsize=10)
axT.set_xlim(-0.02, 1.02); axT.set_ylim(0, 1)
axT.set_yticks([]); axT.set_title("An interval has probability = its length")

# Bottom: shrinking intervals around a point
axB.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((0, 0), 1, 1, facecolor="none", edgecolor="black", lw=1.3))
c = 0.40
for k, eps in enumerate([0.20, 0.10, 0.04]):
    axB.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((c, 0.15 + 0.25 * k), eps, 0.18,
                  facecolor=SETB, alpha=0.5 + 0.15 * k))
    axB.text(c + eps + 0.02, 0.24 + 0.25 * k, fr"$\varepsilon={eps}$", fontsize=8.5, va="center")
axB.axvline(c, color=LINE, lw=1.6)
axB.text(c, -0.12, "$c=0.4$", ha="center", color=LINE, fontsize=10)
axB.set_xlim(-0.02, 1.02); axB.set_ylim(0, 1)
axB.set_yticks([]); axB.set_title(r"A point is squeezed: $P(\{c\})\leq\varepsilon$ for all $\varepsilon>0$, so $=0$")
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"If every individual outcome has probability zero, how can the dart land anywhere at
all?"* Because **probability-zero is not the same as impossible** in a continuous space.
Each point is a possible outcome (the dart does land on exactly one), yet each carries
probability $0$; the probability mass is spread across the continuum so thinly that no
single point holds any of it, but every interval of positive length holds its share. The
resolution hinges on (A3) summing only *countably* — you cannot rebuild the length by
adding up uncountably many point-probabilities. This distinction (the difference between
$P(\cdot)=0$ and $\varnothing$) is the conceptual price of continuous probability, and it
is exactly why [PROB-4] describes continuous variables by a *density* over sets rather than
a mass on points.
:::

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-failure}

The axioms are airtight, so "failure" here means the ways people *misuse* them, and the one
genuine boundary where the framework itself needs care.

## Misuse 1 — confusing "disjoint" with "independent" {#sec-disjoint-indep}

The single most common error built on this foundation is conflating two unrelated ideas.
**Disjoint** ($A \cap B = \varnothing$) means the events *cannot both occur*; **independent**
(defined in [PROB-1]) means *one occurring tells you nothing about the other*. They are
nearly opposites: if $A$ and $B$ are disjoint and both have positive probability, then
knowing $A$ occurred tells you $B$ definitely did *not* — the strongest possible
dependence. Disjointness is a fact about the *sets*; independence is a fact about the
*probabilities*. We flag it here, settle it in [PROB-1].

## Misuse 2 — adding probabilities of overlapping events {#sec-misuse-add}

Writing $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$ when $A$ and $B$ overlap double-counts the intersection
and can yield a "probability" above $1$. The fix is inclusion–exclusion @eq-incexc2 every
time the events are not known to be disjoint. @fig-area and @fig-dice are the permanent
reminders: shaded regions, counted once.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Probabilities only add over *disjoint* events, and additivity is only *countable*.** Two
failures hide here. First, adding $P(A)+P(B)$ for overlapping events over-counts — always
check disjointness or use inclusion–exclusion. Second, never try to sum probabilities over
an *uncountable* collection: in a continuous space each point has probability $0$, yet
"summing" the uncountably many zeros to recover the total is not licensed by (A3), which
sums only over sequences $A_1, A_2, \dots$. Mistaking pointwise-zero for total-zero is the
continuous analogue of the disjoint-vs-overlap error.
:::

## The genuine boundary — non-measurable sets {#sec-nonmeasurable}

The one place the *framework itself* strains is the technical fine print we flagged in
@sec-axioms. In an uncountable $\Omega$ like $[0,1]$, one can construct (using the axiom of
choice) bizarre subsets — **Vitali sets** are the classic example — to which *no*
assignment of probability can be consistent with (A1)–(A3) simultaneously with
translation-invariance. The resolution is not to patch the axioms but to **restrict their
domain**: $P$ is defined only on a $\sigma$-algebra of "measurable" events, and the
pathological sets are simply excluded. For all of statistics and data science this costs us
nothing — intervals, their countable unions, and everything built from them are measurable,
and you will never knowingly meet a non-measurable event in practice. But honesty requires
naming the boundary: "assign a probability to *every* subset" is too much to ask of an
infinite space, and **measure theory** is the discipline that says precisely which subsets
are fair game. That theory is above this track's floor; we use only its safe conclusion.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When is the equally-likely (classical) model the right tool, and when is it wrong?"* The
honest summary you should be able to recite:

- **Use the classical model** only when there is a genuine, defensible symmetry making the
  finitely many outcomes interchangeable — fair dice, well-shuffled cards, a balanced
  spinner with equal sectors. Then @eq-classical is exact and counting (the combinatorics of
  [PROB-10]) does all the work.
- **Do not use it** when outcomes are not symmetric (a loaded die, a biased coin, real-world
  events like conversions or failures — @sec-loaded), where you assign the actual masses and
  combine them by the axioms; or when $\Omega$ is infinite/continuous (the dart —
  @sec-dart), where probability is carried by lengths/areas/volumes (densities, [PROB-4])
  and "favorable over total" is meaningless.
- **The deepest caveat:** the model is a *choice*. Picking the sample space and the
  probability assignment is where the modeling judgment lives; the axioms only guarantee
  that *once chosen*, your calculations cannot contradict each other. They make you
  consistent, not correct.
:::

# How you would actually compute with this {#sec-implementation}

The math is primary; this is the secondary, practical note. For a finite $\Omega$, the
entire apparatus is just dictionaries and set operations: represent $\Omega$ as a list or
set of outcomes, an event as a Python `set`, and read the operations straight off
@def-setops — `A | B` is the union, `A & B` the intersection, `Omega - A` the complement.
Probability of an event under equal weights is `len(A) / len(Omega)`; under arbitrary
weights it is `sum(p[w] for w in A)`. We used exactly this in @sec-twodice and @sec-loaded,
and it is how you would sanity-check any hand derivation: enumerate, count, and confirm the
closed-form rule (complement, inclusion–exclusion) agrees with the brute-force count. For
continuous $\Omega$ you switch from counting to integrating lengths/areas — the subject of
[PROB-4]. The library you will eventually reach for (`scipy.stats`, `numpy.random`) is built
on exactly these axioms; knowing them is knowing what those tools are *allowed* to do.

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
This is the ground floor — there is nothing before it. From here the Probability
Foundations track builds upward, and every other track in the series eventually draws on
what we established:

- **[PROB-1 *Conditional Probability, Independence & Bayes' Rule*]** — the immediate next
  step: how $P(A)$ changes when we learn that $B$ occurred. It is built by *restricting the
  sample space* to $B$, so it stands directly on the $\Omega$/event machinery here, and it
  settles the disjoint-vs-independent confusion we flagged in @sec-disjoint-indep.
- **[PROB-2 *Random Variables*]** — the conceptual leap: a random variable is a *function*
  $X : \Omega \to \mathbb{R}$ on the very sample space we just built, turning outcomes into
  numbers so calculus can touch them. The whole rest of probability is functions on
  $\Omega$.
- **[PROB-4 *Continuous Random Variables*]** — picks up the dart of @sec-dart, replacing
  "probability per point" (which is $0$) with a *density* integrated over sets, the
  continuous heir to @eq-uniform.
- **[PROB-10 *Counting: Permutations & Combinations*]** — develops the combinatorics that
  the classical model @eq-classical reduces probability to, in the equally-likely case.

Cross-track, this article is what lets every downstream track honor the Calc-1 floor: the
sample space, events, and axioms are silently assumed by Inference, Regression,
Classification, and the rest, and they all point back here rather than rebuilding them.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The founding source.** A. N. Kolmogorov, *Grundbegriffe der
  Wahrscheinlichkeitsrechnung* (1933), translated as *Foundations of the Theory of
  Probability* — the monograph that gave probability its axioms. Remarkably short and
  readable; §1 is essentially @sec-axioms above.
- **The historical seed.** The 1654 Pascal–Fermat correspondence on the problem of points,
  reprinted in many collections (e.g. F. N. David, *Games, Gods and Gambling*, 1962), where
  the favorable-over-total recipe is born — and where you can see, in hindsight, the
  assumptions it never made explicit.
- **The paradoxes that forced rigor.** Joseph Bertrand's 1889 *Calcul des probabilités*
  posed the "chord of a circle" paradox, where three reasonable ways of choosing a random
  chord give three different answers — the clearest demonstration that "random" and
  "equally likely" need a *defined* sample space before they mean anything.
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *Which subsets are measurable, and
  what is a $\sigma$-algebra?* — the measure-theoretic foundation (@sec-nonmeasurable),
  above this track's floor. *How does conditioning rebuild the sample space?* — [PROB-1].
  *Why does the long-run frequency in @fig-freq actually converge?* — the Law of Large
  Numbers, an Inference-track theorem built from the Chebyshev inequality of [PROB-19]. We
  supplied the inequality-free *interpretation* here; the limit theorem is owned downstream.
